q: What is the core operating principle of Kubernetes?
a: Declarative desired state + reconciliation: you declare what you want (in YAML), and controllers continuously drive actual state toward desired. This is why K8s self-heals — a dead Pod makes actual < desired, so a controller recreates it.

q: Which component is the cluster's single front door, and which stores all cluster state?
a: The kube-apiserver is the front door (everything talks to it); etcd is the key-value store holding all cluster state. Only the apiserver reads/writes etcd.

q: What do the scheduler, controller-manager, kubelet, and kube-proxy each do?
a: Scheduler assigns Pods to Nodes; controller-manager runs the reconciliation loops; kubelet (on each node) makes the runtime run assigned Pods and reports health; kube-proxy programs node networking so Service VIPs reach Pods.

q: Why do you almost never create a bare Pod?
a: Pods are mortal and disposable — they aren't repaired in place, get new IPs, and aren't recreated if their node dies. You create a controller (Deployment) that manages Pods for you.

q: What does a Deployment manage, and what does it give you?
a: A Deployment manages ReplicaSets (which keep N identical Pods alive) and provides declarative rolling updates and rollbacks — the standard way to run a stateless app.

q: How are Kubernetes objects connected to each other?
a: Via labels (key/value tags on objects) and selectors (label queries). A Deployment owns Pods whose labels match its selector; Services target Pods the same way. selector.matchLabels must match the Pod template's labels.

q: What happens during a rolling update, and why is rollback instant?
a: The Deployment creates a new ReplicaSet and gradually scales it up while scaling the old one down (respecting maxUnavailable/maxSurge). The old ReplicaSet is kept at 0, so `kubectl rollout undo` just scales it back up — instant rollback.

q: When do you use a DaemonSet, Job/CronJob, or StatefulSet instead of a Deployment?
a: DaemonSet: one Pod per node (agents/log collectors). Job/CronJob: run to completion / on a schedule. StatefulSet: stateful apps needing stable identity + per-Pod persistent storage (databases).

q: Why can't you connect directly to a Pod's IP, and what solves it?
a: Pods are ephemeral and get new IPs constantly. A Service provides a stable virtual IP + DNS name in front of label-selected Pods and load-balances to the healthy ones (its Endpoints).

q: Compare ClusterIP, NodePort, and LoadBalancer Service types.
a: ClusterIP (default): reachable only inside the cluster (internal services). NodePort: opens a static port on every node's IP. LoadBalancer: provisions an external cloud load balancer with a public IP — the standard external exposure on cloud.

q: How does service discovery work in Kubernetes?
a: Cluster DNS (CoreDNS) gives every Service a name — <service>, <service>.<namespace>, or the FQDN. Apps connect by hostname (e.g. 'db' or 'api.prod'), no IPs needed.

q: What does Ingress do, and what's the gotcha?
a: Ingress provides one entry point that routes external HTTP(S) by host/path to many Services, with TLS. Gotcha: an Ingress resource does nothing without an Ingress controller (ingress-nginx, Traefik, etc.) installed to implement the rules.

q: What's the difference between a ConfigMap and a Secret?
a: Both decouple config from the image and can be consumed as env vars or mounted files. ConfigMaps hold non-sensitive config; Secrets hold sensitive values (passwords, tokens, TLS certs).

q: Why is 'Secrets are base64-encoded' not 'Secrets are secure'?
a: Base64 is encoding, not encryption — by default Secrets are only base64 in etcd, readable by anyone with etcd/API access. Harden with encryption at rest, tight RBAC, and keep real Secrets out of Git (Sealed Secrets / External Secrets / Vault).

q: Explain PVC, PV, and StorageClass.
a: PVC = a Pod's storage request (size + access mode). PV = an actual piece of storage. StorageClass = a storage type enabling dynamic provisioning, so a PVC auto-creates a matching PV. (Request / supply / vending machine.)

q: Why do databases use a StatefulSet rather than a Deployment?
a: StatefulSets give each Pod a stable identity (db-0, db-1) and, via volumeClaimTemplates, its own PVC that follows it across restarts — so each replica always reattaches to its own disk. Plus ordered rollout.

q: What's the difference between a liveness and a readiness probe?
a: Liveness: is the app alive? Failing it RESTARTS the container. Readiness: is it ready to serve now? Failing it removes the Pod from Service endpoints (no traffic) WITHOUT restarting. Don't conflate them.

q: What's the difference between resource requests and limits?
a: requests = the guaranteed reservation the scheduler uses to place the Pod. limits = the hard ceiling (enforced as cgroups). Exceed memory limit → OOMKilled; exceed CPU limit → throttled (not killed).

q: What are the three QoS classes and which is evicted first?
a: Guaranteed (requests==limits) — evicted last; Burstable (requests<limits) — middle; BestEffort (no requests/limits) — evicted FIRST under node memory pressure. Use Guaranteed for critical workloads.

q: What does an HPA do, and what does CPU-based HPA require?
a: The HorizontalPodAutoscaler adds/removes Pod replicas based on metrics (e.g. keep avg CPU ~70%). CPU-based scaling REQUIRES CPU requests, because utilization is measured against the request.

q: What is the relationship between OpenShift and Kubernetes?
a: OpenShift IS Kubernetes — a certified distribution — plus an enterprise layer (security guardrails, developer tooling, bundled router/registry/monitoring/CI). Standard K8s objects and kubectl all still work.

q: What is an OpenShift Route, and how does it relate to Ingress?
a: A Route is OpenShift's native object for exposing HTTP(S) externally via a built-in HAProxy router, with easy edge/passthrough/reencrypt TLS. Ingress objects also work (OpenShift creates Routes for them).

q: A public image works on plain K8s but fails with permission errors on OpenShift. Why?
a: OpenShift refuses to run containers as root by default and assigns a random high UID, enforced by Security Context Constraints (SCCs). Fix: build images that run as an arbitrary non-root UID (group-writable, GID 0, no privileged ports).

q: What do S2I and BuildConfig provide in OpenShift?
a: Source-to-Image builds a runnable container image from source code + a builder image with no Dockerfile; BuildConfig defines the build and ImageStream tracks versions and can trigger redeploys. `oc new-app` goes from Git to running Pod.

q: Deployment vs DeploymentConfig in OpenShift — which should you use?
a: Prefer the standard Kubernetes Deployment for new work. DeploymentConfig is OpenShift's older object (with triggers/hooks) you'll still see in legacy projects.

q: Why does etcd run an odd number of members (3 or 5)?
a: etcd replicates via the Raft consensus algorithm and needs a quorum (a majority) to accept writes. An odd count gives a clean majority and maximizes fault tolerance per node: 3 members tolerate 1 failure, 5 tolerate 2.

q: Before a risky cluster change, how do you make etcd recoverable, and how?
a: Take a snapshot: `ETCDCTL_API=3 etcdctl snapshot save snap.db` (with the CA/cert/key). Restore with `etcdctl snapshot restore`. Losing etcd loses the whole cluster, so back it up before upgrades — managed clusters do this for you.

q: What are the scheduler's two phases when placing a Pod?
a: Filtering — eliminate nodes the Pod can't run on (insufficient resources, untolerated taints, unmet affinity), leaving feasible nodes. Scoring — rank those feasible nodes and pick the highest. It's implemented as the pluggable Scheduling Framework.

q: What's the difference between a taint and node affinity?
a: A taint lives on the node and repels Pods — only Pods that tolerate it may land (the node opts out by default; used to reserve nodes). Node affinity lives on the Pod and attracts it toward matching nodes (the Pod opts in). They're inverses.

q: What do the taint effects NoSchedule, PreferNoSchedule, and NoExecute do?
a: NoSchedule: block new Pods that don't tolerate the taint. PreferNoSchedule: soft — avoid if possible. NoExecute: block new Pods AND evict already-running Pods that don't tolerate it.

q: What does a PodDisruptionBudget protect against, and what kind of disruption?
a: A PDB caps how many replicas can be down at once (minAvailable / maxUnavailable) during VOLUNTARY disruptions — `kubectl drain`, node upgrades, autoscaler scale-down must respect it. It does not protect against involuntary events like a node crash.

q: Name the four RBAC object types and what each does.
a: Role (namespaced) and ClusterRole (cluster-wide) list allowed verbs on resources. RoleBinding and ClusterRoleBinding grant a (Cluster)Role to subjects — users, groups, or ServiceAccounts. Pods authenticate as a ServiceAccount.

q: Why can a NetworkPolicy be applied yet block nothing?
a: NetworkPolicy is only enforced by a CNI that implements it (Calico, Cilium). Plugins like plain flannel ignore it — the object exists but no traffic is filtered. Always verify your CNI enforces policies before relying on them.

q: How do you create a default-deny-ingress for a namespace with NetworkPolicy?
a: Apply a policy with an empty podSelector (selects all Pods) and policyTypes: [Ingress]. Because the model is allow-list, once a Pod is selected, any ingress not explicitly allowed is denied — so an empty allow-list denies all inbound.

q: Compare HPA, VPA, and the Cluster Autoscaler.
a: HPA scales the replica count on metrics (CPU/memory/custom). VPA adjusts a Pod's CPU/memory requests/limits to fit usage. Cluster Autoscaler adds/removes nodes when Pods are unschedulable or nodes are underused. Don't point HPA and VPA at the same resource.

q: What is the difference between a CRD and an operator?
a: A CustomResourceDefinition teaches the apiserver a new object kind — on its own it just stores YAML. An operator = CRD + a custom controller that reconciles it, encoding operational knowledge (provision, back up, fail over) for a stateful app.

q: What's the difference between a mutating and a validating admission webhook?
a: Both run after authn/authz but before the object is persisted. Mutating webhooks modify the object (inject a sidecar, set defaults); validating webhooks accept or reject it (enforce policy, e.g. ban :latest images). Mutating runs first.
